title: What Is This Government Going to Do Now?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil reading copy below was checked against the source-page images. A stray OCR-only Latin line was removed, the historical meeting date was restored as 5.4.2000, the closing date as 7-7-2014, and clear OCR character and spacing errors were corrected. The canonical source document remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In a statement issued on 23 June 2014, Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa garlanded herself with praise, declaring that just as she had established Tamil Nadu's rights in the Cauvery and Mullaperiyar river-water disputes, she would similarly establish the State's rights in the Palar dispute. On the 27th itself, I asked: the Chief Minister claims that she established Tamil Nadu's rights in the Cauvery and Mullaperiyar disputes, but has the Cauvery dispute been resolved? Is water reaching Tamil Nadu's farmers properly? Is cultivation proceeding as it should? Have Tamil Nadu's rights in the Mullaperiyar dispute been fully established? There has been no answer to this day.

Likewise, in my statement of 27 June 2014, I referred to Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy's speech in the Kerala Legislative Assembly. He said that the Mullaperiyar, Parambikulam, Thunakkadavu and Peruvaripallam dams had been listed as belonging to Tamil Nadu until 2009, but that following Kerala's strong objection, the record was changed in 2012 to show those four dams as belonging to Kerala. I asked: who, then, established this right?

Responding to my question, the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister attacked me sharply. She said a footnote stated that the four dams were operated and maintained by Tamil Nadu, and accused me of issuing a statement in haste. Even before I could respond to Jayalalithaa, Kerala Chief Minister Oommen Chandy himself told reporters on 29 June 2014: "Tamil Nadu is wrong to claim that these four dams belong to it. Even though Tamil Nadu operates and maintains them, it cannot claim ownership. Under the terms of the agreement between the two States, Tamil Nadu maintains and administers the Mullaperiyar, Parambikulam, Peruvaripallam and Thunakkadavu dams. In the National Register of Large Dams, all four are recorded as belonging to Kerala. There is no question of compromise on this issue."

Pointing to the Kerala Chief Minister's reply, I asked Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa to answer him instead of venting her anger at me. But, as usual, Jayalalithaa fell silent. She gave no answer.

Meanwhile, when the Kerala Chief Minister was in Delhi, a *Dinamani* reporter asked him why the Kerala government had filed a review petition in the Mullaperiyar matter without accepting the Supreme Court's judgment. He replied: "The Kerala government's position on Mullaperiyar has always been clear. If the water level is raised from 136 feet to 142 feet in accordance with the Supreme Court judgment, five riparian districts around the dam will be affected. In our review petition, we have urged that Kerala's concerns about the dam's safety be taken into account. We must see what decision the Supreme Court takes. If the outcome is not favourable to Kerala, we will consult legal experts and decide whether to seek, through the President, a clarification of the Supreme Court's judgment."
The Kerala Chief Minister has also held consultations with senior advocate Harish Salve and others who represent Kerala before the Supreme Court in the Mullaperiyar case.

The Supreme Court directed that the water level in the Mullaperiyar dam be raised. To implement that judgment, the Union government appointed a supervisory committee on the first of this month. Even before the committee began functioning, the Kerala government asked the Supreme Court to admit its review petition and to stay the committee's work until a decision was reached. Yet the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister continues to sing her own praises, claiming that she established Tamil Nadu's rights in the Mullaperiyar dispute.

The same thing happened last year when the Supreme Court ordered that the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal's final award be published in the Gazette. Jayalalithaa immediately brought farmers to Thanjavur, held a celebration in her honour and claimed that she had established Tamil Nadu's rights in the Cauvery dispute.

The Kerala government has repeatedly advanced one argument: that the Mullaperiyar dam is unsafe. It says the same even now. Work to strengthen the dam began as early as 1980 and was completed in 1998. To test the dam's strength, the Union government appointed a twelve-member expert committee from the northern States under Dr S. S. Brar. The committee inspected the Mullaperiyar dam in person with the appropriate instruments and reported that the dam was strong and could hold water up to 145 feet. But the Kerala government refused to accept that report.

Between 1996 and 1999 alone, during the DMK government, I wrote many letters to the Kerala government about the Mullaperiyar issue. To give the dates: 2-12-1996, 29-3-1997, 7-11-1997, 3-12-1997, 9-12-1997, 10-12-1997, 11-12-1997, 15-12-1997, 8-1-1998, 20-1-1998, 24-2-1998, 3-4-1998, 28-5-1998, 13-6-1998, 2-7-1998, 13-8-1998, 21-8-1998, 30-9-1998, 13-10-1998, 24-5-1999, 6-7-1999, 14-7-1999, 22-7-1999 and 5-8-1999. On 5 April 2000, I went to Thiruvananthapuram and held talks with the Kerala Chief Minister. On 19 May 2000, my brother Duraimurugan, who was then a minister, and I attended talks between the Chief Ministers of the two States in Delhi, in the presence of the Union Water Resources Minister.

The Union government later sent an eleven-member committee headed by D. K. Mittal to inspect the dam again from different angles. After its tests, this committee too reported to the Union government: "The dam is very strong; only a small quantity of seepage water is coming through." Kerala refused to accept this as well.

The Kerala government then called in personnel from the Kochi naval base. They submerged themselves in the reservoir and conducted several tests, including echo tests and underwater-camera inspection. They too reported: "The dam is strong, but there are fifteen feet of silt and sludge."

As early as 2006, the Supreme Court ordered that the Mullaperiyar water level be raised to 142 feet. The Kerala government refused to accept that order. Immediately after the judgment, Kerala enacted a new law that prevented its implementation. Tamil Nadu appealed to the Supreme Court against this.

In this case, during the DMK government, following the Supreme Court's advice on 25 September 2006 and the decision taken at the all-party meeting on 23 October 2006, I, as Tamil Nadu Chief Minister, held talks in Delhi with Kerala Chief Minister Achuthanandan on 29 November 2006 in the presence of Union Water Resources Minister Saifuddin Soz. Minister-level talks between Tamil Nadu and Kerala followed in Delhi on 18 December 2006, again in the Union minister's presence. No consensus was reached in those talks.

Later, as suggested by the Prime Minister of India, a meeting of the Tamil Nadu and Kerala Chief Ministers was held in Delhi on 19 December 2007 in the presence of the Union Water Resources Minister. No agreement emerged there either.

After hearing arguments from 20 January 2010 to 17 February 2010, a five-judge Constitution Bench directed on 18 February 2010 that a five-member Empowered Committee be constituted under retired Chief Justice of India Dr A. S. Anand. Justice A. R. Lakshmanan and former Supreme Court judge K. T. Thomas of Kerala were among its members. The committee inspected the dam and informed the Supreme Court that it was extremely strong. Following this, a Bench headed by Chief Justice of India R. M. Lodha recently ordered that the water level be raised to 142 feet.

Even after that, the Kerala government has continued to say that the dam is weak and has now filed a review petition in the Supreme Court. Meanwhile, in an interview two days ago, former Justice K. T. Thomas said: "The Mullaperiyar dam is extremely strong. It is the safest dam in India. Yet some people are creating panic among Kerala's people by claiming that the dam is weak."

Even so, what is the Tamil Nadu government going to do? The Kerala Chief Minister and Kerala government are in Delhi discussing the matter with lawyers. The Kerala Chief Minister himself has told reporters there that if the Supreme Court does not accept their request in the review petition, they will appeal to the President. Is there a government in Tamil Nadu to worry about all this and deliberate upon it? What is that government doing? Will it merely continue singing its own praises, repeatedly claiming that it alone secured a solution to the Mullaperiyar problem? Will it go on saying that because the people of Tamil Nadu voted for it in large numbers in the parliamentary election, no one has the right to question it?
With affection,
M. K.
7 July 2014
